perturbation.original_text: The contract parties are obligated to preclude d isclosure of confidential information to third part ies. The parties will entrust confidential information solely to those em ployees, consultants and third party companies whi ch are concerned with the processing of the order and whic h are legally or contractually obligated to secrecy .
perturbation.changed_text: The contract parties should endeavor to preclude disclosure of confidential information to third parties. The parties will entrust confidential information to employees, consultants, and third-party companies whom they believe are concerned with the processing of the order and whom they consider obligated to secrecy.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a strict obligation to prevent disclosure. The modification weakens this by using 'should endeavor' which is less forceful and provides wiggle room. Also, changing 'solely to those ... who are legally or contractually obligated' to 'whom they believe are concerned... and whom they consider obligated' introduces a subjective element, weakening the enforceability of ensuring secrecy. The subjective belief is not equivalent to being actually and legally bound to secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: German Law against unfair competition (Gesetz gegen den unlauteren Wettbewerb - UWG) § 17
perturbation.law_explanation: UWG § 17 punishes the unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to 'preclude disclosure' and by making the selection of who is entrusted with secrets based on subjective belief rather than actual legal obligation, the modified text creates a risk of violating UWG § 17, since it lowers the standard of care required to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the obligation to protect confidential information and trade secrets, increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure. The original text imposes a stricter duty to "preclude disclosure" and entrusts information only to those "legally or contractually obligated to secrecy." The changed text softens this to "should endeavor to preclude disclosure" and relies on subjective belief regarding who is concerned with processing and obligated to secrecy. This creates a potential gap in protection, as individuals merely 'believed' to be obligated may not actually be legally bound, thus undermining trade secret protection, as highlighted in the Law Explanation referencing UWG § 17. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines the Uniform Trade Secrets Act as providing 'states with a legal framework for improved trade secret protection to industry,' indicating a legal requirement for robust protection which the modified text weakens.

perturbation.original_text: At the end of the contract the receiving party s hall return the confidential information and any po ssibly extant copies after receipt of a written request, provided that this is technically feasible and does not vio late the retention obligations.
perturbation.changed_text: At the end of the contract, the receiving party should make an effort to return the confidential information and any possibly extant copies after receipt of a written request, considering technical feasibility and retention obligations.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the recipient 'shall return' the information. The changed text replaces this with 'should make an effort to return', making the return discretionary. By adding 'considering' before 'technical feasibility and retention obligations,' these become factors to consider rather than strict limitations.
perturbation.law_explanation: BGB § 280 establishes a general claim for damages due to a breach of duty. The initial 'shall return' creates a clear contractual duty. The changed text's 'should make an effort' weakens this, potentially leading to a breach of contract claim if the information isn't returned. By weakening a clear duty, the clause is now ambiguous enough that it may be deemed as unenforceable, allowing the parties to avoid that duty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: de BGB Full citation: Civil Code in the version promulgated on 2 January 2002 (Federal Law Gazette [ Bundesgesetzblatt ] I page 42, 2909; 2003 I page 738), last amended by Article 1 of the Act of 10 August 2021 (Federal Law Gazette I p. 3515). This statute serves to implement the following directives: 1. Council Directive 76/207/EEC of 9 February 1976 on the implementation of the principle of equal treatment for men and women as regards access to employment, vocational training and promotion, and working conditions (OJ L 39 of 14 February 1976, p. 40), 2. Council Directive 85/577/EEC of 20 December 1985 to protect the consumer in respect of contracts negotiated away from business premises (OJ L 372 of 31 December 1985, p. 31), 4. Council Directive 87/102/EEC for the approximation of the laws, regulations and administrative provisions of the Member States concerning consumer credit (OJ L 42 of 12 February 1987, p. 48, last amended by Directive 98/7/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 16 February 1998 amending Directive 87/102/EEC for the approximation of the laws, regulations and administrative provisions of the Member States concerning consumer credit (OJ L 101 of 1 April 1998, p. 17), 5. Council Directive 90/314/EEC of 13 June 1990 on package travel, package holidays and package tours (OJ L 158 of 23 June 1990, p. 59), 6. Council Directive 93/13/EEC of 5 April 1993 on unfair terms in consumer contracts (OJ L 95 of 21 April 1993, p. 29), 7. Directive 94/47/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 October 1994 on the protection of purchasers in respect of certain aspects of contracts relating to the purchase of the right to use immovable properties on a timeshare basis (OJ L 280 of 29 October 1994, p. 82), 8. Directive 97/5/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27 January 1997 on cross-border credit transfers (OJ L 43 of 14 February 1997, p. 25), 9. Articles 3 to 5 of Directive 98/26/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 19 May 1998 on settlement finality in payment and securities settlement systems (OJ L 166 of 11 June 1998, p. 45), 11. Directive 1999/44/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 25 May 1999 on certain aspects of the sale of consumer goods and associated guarantees (OJ L 171 of 7 July 1999, p. 12), 12. Articles 10, 11 and 18 of Directive 2000/31/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 8 June 2000 on certain legal aspects of information society services, in particular electronic commerce, in the Internal Market (“Directive on electronic commerce” OJ L 178 of 17 July 2000, p. 1), 13. Directive 2000/35/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 29 June 2000 on combating late payment in commercial transactions (OJ L 200 of 8 August 2000, p. 35). table of contents Book 1 General part table of contents Division 1 Persons table of contents Title 1 Natural persons, consumers, traders table of contents Section 1 Beginning of legal capacity The legal capacity of a human being begins on the completion of birth. table of contents Section 2 Beginning of majority Majority begins at the age of eighteen. table of contents Sections 3 – 6 (repealed) table of contents Section 7 Residence; establishment and termination (1) A person who settles permanently in a place establishes their residence in that place. (2) There may be a residence in more than one place at the same time. (3) Residence is terminated if the person abandons the place of residence with the intention of giving it up. table of contents Sect
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that the original text mandates the recipient 'shall return' the information, while the changed text weakens this to 'should make an effort to return'. The law explanation supports this by stating BGB § 280 establishes a claim for damages due to a breach of duty and the changed text may lead to a breach of contract. Therefore, the original text created a clear contractual duty which the changed text weakens, potentially making it unenforceable and creating a contradiction. There is no specific snippet available to determine the validity of this statement, only the law explanation and the perturbation explanation to analyze.

perturbation.original_text: If the recipient is forced to expose the confide ntial information due to a legal regulation, a lega lly valid judgement or a non-disputable official decision or decree in part or in whole, it shall immediately inform the o wner of the confidential information in writing so that the own er can take measures to protect the confidential in formation.
perturbation.changed_text: If the recipient is required to expose the confidential information due to a legal request, a judgement, or an official request, it should consider informing the owner of the confidential information so that the owner might take measures.
perturbation.explanation: The change weakens the 'forced to expose' to 'required to expose' broadening it to potentially include voluntary cooperation. Replacing 'shall immediately inform' with 'should consider informing' makes notification discretionary. The phrase 'legal regulation' is replaced with 'legal request', a much broader term that may not have the same legal standing. Finally, changing 'can take measures' to 'might take measures' weakens the likelihood of corrective actions being undertaken by the other party.
perturbation.law_explanation: StGB § 203 criminalizes the unauthorized disclosure of business secrets. The original clause requires immediate notification to allow the owner to protect the information. By making notification discretionary, the modified clause reduces the ability of the information owner to mitigate the harm from the disclosure, potentially increasing the severity of the damage and raising the risk of a successful prosecution under StGB § 203.

Arzt, Zahnarzt, Tierarzt, Apotheker oder Angehörigen eines anderen Heilberufs, der für die Berufsausübung oder die Führung der Berufsbezeichnung eine staatlich geregelte Ausbildung erfordert, 2. Berufspsychologen mit staatlich anerkannter wissenschaftlicher Abschlußprüfung, 3. Organ oder Mitglied eines Organs einer Wirtschaftsprüfungs-, Buchprüfungs- oder einer Berufsausübungsgesellschaft von Steuerberatern und Steuerbevollmächtigten, einer Berufsausübungsgesellschaft von Rechtsanwälten oder europäischen niedergelassenen Rechtsanwälten oder einer Berufsausübungsgesellschaft von Patentanwälten oder niedergelassenen europäischen Patentanwälten im Zusammenhang mit der Beratung und Vertretung der Wirtschaftsprüfungs-, Buchprüfungs- oder Berufsausübungsgesellschaft im Bereich der Wirtschaftsprüfung, Buchprüfung oder Hilfeleistung in Steuersachen oder ihrer rechtsanwaltlichen oder patentanwaltlichen Tätigkeit, 4. Angehörigen eines Unternehmens der privaten Kranken-, Unfall- oder Lebensversicherung oder einer privatärztlichen, steuerberaterlichen oder anwaltl
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts StGB § 203 by weakening the obligation to report a potential disclosure of confidential information. The original text mandates immediate written notification ('shall immediately inform'), allowing the owner to protect the information, while the modified text makes notification discretionary ('should consider informing'). StGB § 203 aims to prevent the unauthorized disclosure of business secrets ('Wer unbefugt ein fremdes Geheimnis...offenbart...'). By reducing the likelihood of notification, the modified clause reduces the owner's ability to prevent or mitigate damage from disclosure. The replaced phrases significantly weaken the legal protection afforded to business secrets, creating a contradiction of the legal standard.

perturbation.original_text: All confidential information remains the propert y of the disclosing contract party.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential information is generally considered the property of the disclosing contract party.
perturbation.explanation: The original states unequivocally that all confidential information 'remains the property' of the disclosing party. The change introduces ambiguity by saying the information is 'generally considered' the property of the disclosing party, which implies there may be exceptions, making it unclear under what circumstances the information is not considered the disclosing party's property.
perturbation.law_explanation: BGB § 903 defines the rights of the owner of a thing (Sache). The original text clearly establishes ownership of the confidential information. By stating that it is 'generally considered' the property, the amended text contradicts the clear and absolute right of disposal granted to the owner under BGB § 903, opening the door to arguments that the receiving party has certain rights or claims to the information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 903 BGB - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB) § 903 Befugnisse des Eigentümers Der Eigentümer einer Sache kann, soweit nicht das Gesetz oder Rechte Dritter entgegenstehen, mit der Sache nach Belieben verfahren und andere von jeder Einwirkung ausschließen. Der Eigentümer eines Tieres hat bei der Ausübung seiner Befugnisse die besonderen Vorschriften zum Schutz der Tiere zu beachten. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular
perturbation.justification: The original text states 'All confidential information remains the property of the disclosing contract party,' establishing clear ownership. The changed text weakens this by stating confidential information is 'generally considered the property,' implying exceptions. This contradicts BGB § 903, which grants the owner of a thing (Sache) the right to 'mit der Sache nach Belieben verfahren und andere von jeder Einwirkung ausschließen' (deal with the thing as he pleases and exclude others from any influence). The 'generally considered' language introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing the receiving party to claim rights or influence over the confidential information, thus undermining the owner's exclusive rights under BGB § 903.

perturbation.original_text: To the extent that technical drawings of certain third-party companies are not to be made known, th e buyer must point this fact out on its own initiative.
perturbation.changed_text: If the buyer is reasonably aware that technical drawings of certain third-party companies should not be disclosed, the buyer may consider mentioning this.
perturbation.explanation: The original text places a clear and direct obligation on the buyer to point out if third-party drawings are not to be made known. The changed text weakens this by saying 'if the buyer is reasonably aware' (introducing a subjective element) and replacing 'must point this fact out' with 'may consider mentioning this' (making the action discretionary).
perturbation.contradicted_law: German Law against unfair competition (Gesetz gegen den unlauteren Wettbewerb - UWG) § 4 Nr. 3
perturbation.law_explanation: UWG § 4 Nr. 3 prohibits the betrayal of business secrets. The original text placed a responsibility on the buyer to protect the trade secrets contained in third-party drawings by disclosing that they should not be shared. The changed text turns this into a discretionary action. Therefore the new clause does not take adequate measures to protect the business secrets as needed in accordance with the UWG.

Act against Unfair Competition (Gesetz gegen den unlauteren Wettbewerb – UWG) Full citation: Act against Unfair Competition in the version published on 3 March 2010 (Federal Law Gazette I, p. 254), as last amended by Article 13 of the Act of 8 October 2023 (Federal Law Gazette 2023 I No. 272) This Act serves to implement Directive 2005/29/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 11 May 2005 concerning unfair business-to-consumer commercial practices in the internal market and amending Council Directive 84/450/EEC, Directives 97/7/EC, 98/27/EC and 2002/65/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council and Regulation (EC) No. 2006/2004 of the European Parliament and of the Council (OJ L 149, 11.6.2005, p. 22; rectified in OJ L 253, 25.9.2009, p. 18), as well as Directive 2006/114/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 12 December 2006 concerning misleading and comparative advertising (codified version) (OJ L 376, 27.12.2006, p. 21). This Act further serves to implement Article 13 of Directive 2002/58/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 12 July 2002 concerning the processing of personal data and the protection of privacy in the electronic communications sector (OJ L 201, 31.7.2002, p. 37), as last amended by Article 2 no. 7 of Directive 2009/136/EC (OJ L 337, 18.12.2009, p. 11). The obligations under Directive 98/34/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 22 June 1998 laying down a procedure for the provision of information in the field of technical standards and regulations (OJ L 204, 21.7.1998, p. 37), as last amended by Directive 2006/96/EC (OJ L 363, 20.12.2006, p. 81), have been complied with. table of contents Chapter 1 General provisions table of contents Section 1 Purpose of the Act; scope (1) This Act serves the protection of competitors, consumers and other market participants against unfair commercial practices. At the same time, it protects the interests of the public in undistorted competition. (2) Provisions regulating specific aspects of unfair commercial practices prevail over the provisions of this Act when it comes to assessing whether an unfair co
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the intent of UWG § 4 Nr. 3 regarding the protection of business secrets. The original text creates a direct obligation for the buyer to disclose if technical drawings should not be shared. The changed text weakens this obligation, making it discretionary and contingent on the buyer's awareness. The Law Explanation indicates this shift fails to adequately protect business secrets as required by the UWG. Scraped Law Snippet 2 generally outlines the purpose of the Act against Unfair Competition which is to protect 'competitors, consumers and other market participants against unfair commercial practices' indicating the importance of protecting business secrets. The original text aligns more closely with the protective intent of the UWG.
